name: Quadrantids
description: One of the strongest annual meteor showers, the Quadrantids can produce over 100 meteors per hour at peak. Unlike most showers, the peak window is very narrow — just 6–12 hours — requiring precise timing. Known for producing bright, long-duration fireballs.
historicalStorms: Consistent high rates since discovery in 1825. Rates have exceeded 200 ZHR in exceptional years. The narrow peak and northern hemisphere geometry mean it is less observed than comparable showers.
historicalVisibility: Best visibility in the Northern Hemisphere; the radiant rises after midnight. The shower originates near the extinct constellation Quadrans Muralis, now within Boötes.
viewingTips: Face northeast after midnight local time. The radiant rises low so the best viewing is in the hours before dawn. Bundle up as early January is cold in the northern hemisphere. Allow 20–30 minutes for dark adaptation.

name: Lyrids
description: One of the oldest known meteor showers, the Lyrids have been observed for over 2,700 years. The stream produces swift, bright meteors that occasionally leave persistent glowing trains. Sporadic fireballs and outbursts make it unpredictable and exciting.
historicalStorms: Major outbursts recorded in 1803 (700 ZHR), 1922, 1945, and 1982. The 1803 outburst over the eastern US was described as 'fire falling from the sky.' Outbursts occur roughly every 60 years.
historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres, but significantly better from the Northern Hemisphere. Brightness of individual meteors compensates for the moderate rate.
viewingTips: Look towards Vega in the constellation Lyra, which rises in the northeast after 10 PM. Best viewing is between midnight and dawn. Outburst years can see rates jump to 100+ ZHR, so this shower is always worth watching.

name: Eta Aquariids
description: Debris from the most famous comet in history, Halley's Comet, creates this spectacular May shower. The Eta Aquariids are known for very fast meteors and long, glowing trains that can persist for several minutes. Particularly favored in the Southern Hemisphere.
historicalStorms: Associated with Halley's Comet since 1868. The shower benefits from high activity during Halley's perihelion passages. Southern hemisphere observers can regularly see 30–60 ZHR from dark sites.
historicalVisibility: Southern Hemisphere observers see significantly higher rates (up to 50 ZHR) as the radiant climbs higher in the sky. Northern hemisphere observers typically see 10–30 ZHR from lower northern latitudes.
viewingTips: The radiant rises just before dawn, so the optimal window is the 1–2 hours before sunrise. In the Southern Hemisphere, face northeast and look wide — the fast meteors leave long, luminous trails. May nights are mild in most locations, making for comfortable viewing.

name: Southern Delta Aquariids
description: A consistent, reliable summer shower producing medium-speed meteors. The Southern Delta Aquariids peak broadly, meaning good rates persist for several nights. The shower overlaps with the early Perseids, creating an unusually active late-July sky.
historicalStorms: No historic major outbursts, but the shower has been reliably productive since its formal identification in the mid-20th century. The broad peak allows multiple nights of quality viewing.
historicalVisibility: Particularly good for Southern Hemisphere and equatorial observers, where the radiant rises high in the sky. Northern mid-latitude observers see moderate rates with the radiant low in the south.
viewingTips: Look south in the pre-dawn hours. The radiant sits near the star Skat (delta Aquarii). Combine viewing with early Perseid activity for an action-packed night. Summer nights mean no need for heavy clothing.

name: Perseids
description: The Perseids are the most popular meteor shower in the world — reliably producing up to 100 meteors per hour under dark skies, peaking on warm August nights when observing conditions are pleasant. Known for bright, colorful fireballs and long-lasting streaks.
historicalStorms: Major outbursts in 1991, 1992, and 1993 (rates exceeding 500 ZHR). The 2016 outburst reached 150–200 ZHR. The next predicted enhanced return is around 2028 as Earth passes through denser filaments.
historicalVisibility: Universally well-placed for Northern Hemisphere observers. Summer nights mean comfortable temperatures and no school nights. The shower has been observed since at least 36 AD, historically called 'the tears of Saint Lawrence.'
viewingTips: No equipment needed — just a blanket and a dark field. The radiant in Perseus rises northeast around 11 PM. Watch after midnight when the radiant is high. Point your gaze about 45° away from the radiant to see meteors with longer tails. Dark sky locations show 4× more meteors than suburban settings.

name: Orionids
description: The second annual gift from Halley's Comet, the Orionids produce fast, bright meteors with persistent trains. The shower radiates from near Betelgeuse in Orion, making it one of the most recognizable showers. Ideal for autumn stargazers.
historicalStorms: Outbursts to 50–75 ZHR occurred in 2006–2010, baffling astronomers. The shower returned to typical rates after. Some researchers attribute the outbursts to filament structure in Halley's debris trail.
historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres. The radiant is well-placed from equatorial and both mid-latitude regions. The broad peak means good viewing persists for 5–7 nights around maximum.
viewingTips: The radiant in Orion is best-placed from midnight to dawn. Let your eyes fully adapt to the dark before you start counting. The crisp October air and bright Orion make for a memorable observing session.

name: Leonids
description: Famous for producing some of the most spectacular meteor storms in recorded history, the Leonids typically produce modest rates in non-storm years. Every 33 years, when Comet Tempel-Tuttle returns to the inner solar system, rates can explode to thousands of meteors per minute.
historicalStorms: The 1833 storm produced an estimated 100,000 meteors per hour, described as 'stars falling like rain.' The 1966 storm reached over 150,000 ZHR. The 1999 and 2001 returns saw 3,000–4,000 ZHR. The next major return is expected around 2032–2033.
historicalVisibility: Best observed from the Northern Hemisphere where Leo is higher in the sky. The 1833 storm prompted widespread belief in many communities that the world was ending.
viewingTips: Face east and look toward Leo after midnight. Even in non-storm years, Leonids are swift and bright. Keep an eye on IMO forecasts in years near Comet Tempel-Tuttle's return — a surprise outburst is always possible.

name: Geminids
description: The Geminids are considered the best meteor shower of the year, with rates up to 150 per hour and the rare distinction of originating from an asteroid rather than a comet. The meteors are slower than most, colorful, and often extremely bright — perfect for binocular observation.
historicalStorms: The shower has intensified steadily over the past century as Jupiter's gravity has pulled the debris stream closer to Earth's orbit. Rates in the 1800s were just 10–20 ZHR. The shower is expected to continue growing through the 21st century.
historicalVisibility: One of the few major showers visible well from both hemispheres. The radiant in Gemini is circumpolar from mid-northern latitudes, allowing viewing all night long.
viewingTips: Bundle up — December nights are cold! The radiant in Gemini rises around 9 PM, allowing for earlier viewing than most showers. Geminids are brighter and slower than average, making them easier to spot. Bring hand warmers and a sleeping bag for a full-night session.

name: Ursids
description: Peaking right at the winter solstice, the Ursids are a modest but special shower for dedicated observers. The radiant in Ursa Minor is circumpolar from northern latitudes, meaning it never sets — providing all-night viewing opportunities. Surprise outbursts occasionally boost rates dramatically.
historicalStorms: Unexpected outbursts occurred in 1945 (>100 ZHR), 1986, and 2000. The 2000 outburst reached over 50 ZHR, suggesting complex filament structure in the debris trail. Outbursts are linked to close approaches of Comet 8P/Tuttle.
historicalVisibility: An exclusively northern hemisphere shower — the radiant never rises for Southern Hemisphere observers. Best from polar and sub-arctic regions where the radiant is highest.
viewingTips: Face north toward Polaris to find the radiant in Ursa Minor. The long winter nights and stable radiant altitude allow extended sessions. Thermal layers are essential. The holiday timing makes this a memorable observation — catching a meteor on the solstice is a unique experience.
